2008-01-07.text: Operational Direction of Monetary and Credit Policies in 2008 Monetary Policy Department
. Operational Environment for Monetary and Credit Policies
The inflation target for the years between 2007 and 2009 has been set at a range of 3.0 ± 0.5% in terms of annualized average consumer

o Should inflation deviate from this target range the steps taken to counter this will be decided upon taking into overall consideration the likely duration of its deviation, the time lag of policy transmission, and financial and economic conditions.

o When taking policy measures to address such problems, the authorities will try to minimize the impact on the real economy and financial markets by bring about the gradual convergence of the rate of increase in the CPI with its target from a medium-term
It is expected that the domestic economy for 2008 will maintain the growth level of the previous year, prices will accelerate their upward trend and the current account will register a slight
The real economy is likely to maintain growth at the previous year's level, thanks both to the robust increase of exports and the recovery of domestic demand led by consumption.

The current account is expected to show a modest deficit, owing to the narrowing of the goods account surplus because of the run-up in oil prices and the widening of the services and income account deficit, affected by increased expenditures for overseas travel.

Along with the supply-side factor of high oil prices, the demand side pressures built up by the quickening of economic activity over the last year or so are likely to work through after some time lag, so that the rate of increase in the CPI is forecast to rise above the midpoint of
Note : 1) Figures in parentheses refer to the averages of the respective quarterly rates of increase.
Source : The Bank of Korea, Economic Outlook for 2008, Dec. 5, 2007
3. Financial and Foreign Exchange Markets

Overall financial conditions are expected to be favorable owing to ample market liquidity, although there is the possibility of a worsening of fund-raising conditions for small and medium enterprises with low credit ratings, mainly as a result of the tightening of financial institutions' risk management.
The upward trend of banks' lending is forecast to flatten out somewhat from the previous year.

o Lending to large companies and households will accelerate slightly but the scale of the increase in loans to small and medium enterprises(SMEs), however, is seen to narrow due to the tightening of banks' risk management.
The issue of bonds is likely to increase somewhat from the previous year largely owing to the expanded issue of government bonds.
The appetite for bonds, in contrast, is anticipated to slacken somewhat led by institutional investors such as the National Pension Fund and asset
management companies.
In the foreign exchange market, the scale of net outflow of foreign capital is predicted to expand from the previous year.
o The net current account inflow will decline greatly while capital transactions continue to show a net outflow mainly affected by residents' overseas securities investment.
The total national budget(total expenditure basis including government funds) represents a 7.9 percent expansion over the previous year.
o The scale of the consolidated budget deficit, excluding social security funds accounts, is anticipated to reach 1.1 percent of nominal GDP, slightly less than last year's.
The Bank of Korea will conduct its monetary policy in such a way as to maintain price stability while paying careful attention both to economic growth and financial stability.
The Bank of Korea also plans to firmly establish at an early stage its new monetary policy operational framework and enhance policy operational efficiency.
Interest rate policy will be conducted flexibly with an emphasis on ensuring price stability after overall consideration of economic activity and financial market developments.

o Consumer price inflation is projected to rise above the midpoint of its target range, mainly influenced by high oil prices, as well as by demand-pull pressures generated by the economic upturn now in progress for some time.
o Domestic economic growth is expected to continue at the previous year's level.
There is, however, a high degree of uncertainty, largely due to the possible rises in oil prices and international and domestic financial market unrest.
The Bank of Korea plans to make use of the movements of liquidity indicators as information variables to reflect the inflation risk from a medium-term perspective.

An overall reorganization of the operational framework of monetary policy is to be undertaken in order to heighten the function of the call rate as a market variable and give effective support to interest rate-oriented monetary policy.

o The policy rate will be changed from the overnight call rate target to the Base Rate(the Bank of Korea Base Rate), the rate applied in transactions between the BOK and financial institutions such as RPs.

o Under the revised framework, which is being put in place this year standing facilities(liquidity adjustment loans and deposits) are introduced while the reserve requirements system and the method of conducting open market operations are reorganized.
― 'Loans to Meet Temporary Shortages of Funds' and 'Liquidity Adjustment Loans' are to be abolished.
Multi-faceted efforts are being devoted to ensuring that the new monetary policy operational framework becomes firmly established at
o Prior to its introduction, the various preparatory tasks are being pursued
seamlessly, including the drafting or revision of relevant regulations and the realignment of computer networks and electronic systems.
In the early stages of its implementation, there will be active recourse to the use of complementary open market operations such as overnight RPs if the call rate shows highly unstable movements.
The purchase of the underlying securities eligible for RP sales will continue so that liquidity may be adjusted smoothly.
Quarterly ceilings will be set for MSBs to allow the scale of their issuance to be managed more effectively.
A study will be made on how best to expand the basis of demand
The Bank of Korea will improve the system of support for SMEs, taking into account the availability of bank funds for SMEs.
The Aggregate Credit Ceiling Loans system will be operated with priority being given to regional branch ceiling quotas.
It will also improve step by step the system of guideline ratios for bank lending to SMEs.

The Bank intends to expand the basis for promptly grasping and analyzing financial market developments, so as to cope with the integration of the domestic and international financial markets and the greatly expanded activity of the derivatives markets.
In particular, the derivatives markets will be monitored more vigilantly.
The Bank of Korea is moving to consolidate communication with the financial markets to enhance both the predictability and the transparency of monetary policy.
The Bank will effectively exploit the signalling function of monetary policy statements and continue to broaden feedback channels such as consultation meetings with market participants and experts.
The intention is to conduct monetary policy in a harmonious policy-mix with fiscal, foreign exchange and financial supervision policies.

In response to the increased fragility of the financial market stemming from the over-concentration of domestic funds and international market unrest, the Bank of Korea will constantly monitor latent destabilizing factors and react promptly if signs of instability are detected.
The Bank plans to make appropriate use of the newly introduced standing lending facility as a tool to counter crisis.
The Bank will remain keenly alert to the possibility of reduced lending to SMEs with weak credit standing due to the application (2008) of the New Basel Capital Accord.

Furthermore, the Bank intends to closely monitor the influence of 'the Capital Market and Financial Investment Services Act', which will come into force from 2009, on financial institutions' raising and operation of funds and the consequent flows of funds among them.

The Bank will strengthen the monitoring of the payment and settlement system, paying attention to the possibility of increased settlement risk arising from the drive to expand non-bank financial institutions' participation in the payment and settlement system.
The Bank will ease the bunching of settlements towards the closing time of BOK-Wire by bringing forward the time for the net
settlement of promissory notes, and it will pursue the friction-free construction of BOK-Wire , which is scheduled to be launched during the first half of 2009.

2008-03-07.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to set the Base Rate at 5.00% for the intermeeting period, the same level as the current call rate target.
The domestic economy seems likely to maintain its upward trend.
While private consumption's pace of expansion has slackened, exports continue to post robust growth.
There is, however, a high degree of uncertainty surrounding future economic developments largely due to the international financial market turmoil and the US economic slowdown.
Consumer price inflation continues to show a rapid rise, mainly influenced by high oil prices.
The upward trend of real estate prices remains blunted.
Liquidity is still ample in the financial markets.
Both long-term and short-term market interest rates have maintained downward trends.
Meanwhile, certain financial market price variables have been increasingly volatile, largely in response to the international financial market unrest.

From today, the policy rate that the Monetary Policy Committee sets and announces, has been changed from 'the call rate target' (uncollateralized overnight rate) to 'the Bank of Korea Base Rate' (termed 'the Base Rate' in future).
The Base Rate is the reference rate applied to transactions between the Bank and financial institutions.
Specifically, the Base Rate will be used as the fixed bid rate for 7-day RPs and the minimum bid rate for 7-day reverse RPs in open market operations.
Further, the interest rates on standing facilities, which consist of Liquidity Adjustment Loans and Deposits, will be set by applying spreads fitted around the Base Rate.

Meanwhile, taking into consideration the characteristics of secured 7-day RPs and uncollateralized overnight call transactions, it is the Monetary Policy Committee's opinion that the Base Rate and the call rate target represent equivalents.
Therefore the setting of the Base Rate at the same level as the previous call rate target means that the monetary policy stance remains unchanged.

While the term premium implies that the Base Rate applied to 7-day RPs may be somewhat higher than the overnight call rate, the opposite is in fact the case when the provision of collateral is taken into account.

Korean won against the US dollar.

The upward trend of real estate
prices has been blunted.

Financial market price variables such as exchange rates and
stock prices have shown unstable movements, largely in response to
the spread of international financial market unrest.

Financial
institutions have been tightening their attitude to lending.

The Committee judges that this action should contribute to
soothing the financial market turmoil and to avoiding a severe
contraction of economic activity.

2008-10-27.text:
The Bank of Korea
Monetary Policy Decision

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to lower the Base Rate by 75 basis points from 5.00% to
4.25%.

In a related action, the Committee also lowered the interest rates
on Aggregate Credit Ceiling Loans by 75 basis points from 3.25%
to 2.50%.

(The following is an English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee
statement.)

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to lower the Base Rate by 75 basis points from 5.00% to
4.25%.

The Committee judges that a large cut in the Base Rate is
called for in order to guard securely against the possibility of a
sharp contraction of real economic activity against the backdrop of
the large swings of the exchange rate and stock prices and the
partial seizing-up of the credit markets under the impact of the
international financial market turmoil on domestic markets.

Looking ahead, the Committee will do what is needed to ward
off the risk of a severe slowdown in economic activity while keeping
a vigilant watch on the changes in the upside risk to inflation.

today to lower the Base Rate by 25 basis points from 4.25% to
4.00%.

to 2.25%.

(The following is an English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee
statement.)

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to lower the Base Rate by 25 basis points from 4.25% to

The pace of export
growth has declined owing to the weakening of global economic
growth and domestic demand such as private consumption remains
sluggish.

The downside risk to economic growth is considered to
have been augmented by the knock-on effects of the financial market

Transactions in the real estate market have declined and

Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and
stock prices have continued to show unstable movements.

on the changes in the upside risk to inflation.

2008-12-05.text: BOK to Help Increase Banks' Credit Availability
The Bank of Korea (BOK) held an unscheduled Monetary Policy Committee meeting on Dec 3, 2008, and made the following decisions :
① The BOK will pay interest worth a total of 500 billion won on banks' required reserve deposits.
② The BOK will include bonds issued by the Korea Housing Finance Corporation (KHFC) among the securities traded in open market operations (effective
These measures are focused on supporting expansion of banks' credit availability.
The BOK will in the future consider further measures to restore stability in the financial market, including expansion of liquidity
Banks' BIS capital ratio, an indicator of their financial soundness, has recently fallen dramatically, influenced by the recent domestic and overseas financial market turmoil and the economic slump.

A deterioration in the financial soundness of banks not only leads to deepening economic slump, through a shrinkage of credit supply, but also limits the effectiveness of monetary policy by disrupting smooth operation of the monetary policy transmission lending channel.
The BOK therefore seeks to expand the funds available to banks for lending by enhancing their financial soundness.

To expand banks' credit availablity, The BOK will ① pay interest worth about 500 billion won on banks' required reserve deposits, and ② include bonds issued by the Korea Housing Finance Corporation (KHFC) among the securities traded in open market operations

In accordance with the Bank of Korea Act (Paragraph 3 of Article 55), the BOK will pay a predetermined amount of interest on reserve deposits maintained in the Bank of Korea (hereinafter referred to as "interest-on-reserves")
The total interest-on-reserves to be paid is estimated at about

The total interest-on-reserves is equivalent to the amount of improvements in banks' balances over the coming one year that would be seen if the reserve requirement ratio were cut by 0.8%p the amount by which it was increased in Dec.
This calculation is based on the assumption that banks would show improvements in their balance by fully
utilizing such required reserve deposits, or non-performing assets, as loans.
The calculation used the weighted average for commercial and specialized banks' interest rates on loans of 7.79% as of Oct. 2008, based on newly extended amounts.

This measure is likely to cause an instant improvement in banks' balances, equal to the interest-on-reserves amount, thus leading to increases in their BIS capital ratio and therefore in their credit availabilities.
The BOK will pay interest to financial institutions as follows : ㅇ Institutions to receive interest : all financial institutions required to hold reserve deposits with the BOK

ㅇ Deposits subject to interest : the average annual balance of each financial institution's required reserve deposits for the past one year (Nov. 8, 2007 ~ Nov. 5, 2008) (with any amount exceeding that of required minimum
ㅇ Interest rate : annualized 2.3%*
* This rate will enable the total interest amount to approach 500 billion won.
The BOK will include bonds issued by the Korea Housing Finance Corporation (KHFC) among the securities traded in its open market operations.
ㅇ This measure is likely to ensure the KHFC of smooth procurement of funds, through bond issuance and will enable it to purchase more mortgage-backed bonds from banks using
→ Banks are in consequence likely to see their financial soundness improved and their capability of providing new
□ The expansion in banks' credit availability through these measures is estimated to reach a total of 6.3 trillion won*.
* This will enable banks to maintain their BIS ratios and increase their s 100% risk-weighted loans(corporate loans).
ㅇ Scale of expansion in credit availability through paying interest-on-reserves
ㅇ Scale of expansion in credit availability through expansion of securities traded in open market operations : 1.7 trillion won
□ The BOK will begin paying interest-on-reserves as soon as its confirmation of statistics on required reserve deposits of the banks are completed.
□ The BOK will put the measure for increasing securities traded in open market operations into effect on December 9, after completing the necessary computer system additions.

2008-12-11.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to lower the Base Rate by 100 basis points from 4.00% to 3.00%.
The domestic economy is slowing down rapidly.
The sluggishness of domestic consumption and investment has been deepening and export growth has turned negative.
There is now judged to be a strong likelihood that growth will fall sharply as a result of the worldwide economic slowdown along with the financial market unrest including the credit crunch.

Despite the depreciation of the Korean won against the US dollar, consumer price inflation has been decelerating steadily due to the fall in international oil prices and the impact of the economic slowdown.
In the real estate market, the volume of transactions and prices have continued to decline.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have been increasingly volatile.

Fund-raising conditions for firms have become more difficult mainly due to the continuation of both foreign portfolio investment outflows and the tightening of financial institutions' attitude to lending.
Looking ahead, the Committee will do what is needed to improve liquidity conditions and to ward off the risk of a severe slowdown in economic activity.

2009-01-04.text: Operational Direction of Monetary and Credit Policies in 2009
Monetary Policy Department
Ⅰ. Operational Environment for Monetary and Credit Policies
◆ In the course of 2009, upward price pressures will moderate as the growth rate of domestic economic activity slows sharply while the current account is forecast to show a

□ In the real economy, the rate of GDP growth is expected to be substantially lower than last year's in line with the blunting of export growth by the worldwide economic downturn and the continued stagnation of domestic demand.

□ Despite lackluster exports, the current account is forecast to post a surplus as imports contract even more rapidly than exports under the impact of lower international oil prices and the contraction of domestic demand.

□ The rate of increase in consumer prices is expected to drop considerably owing to the fall in international raw material prices and the waning of demand pull pressures in response to lackluster domestic demand.
2. Financial and Foreign Exchange Markets
◆ Supply of credit is expected to be restricted by continued international financial market turmoil, lackluster economic activity, and the uncertainties surrounding corporate
◆ Foreign currency liquidity conditions are seen to improve thanks partly to the current account surplus.
□ The upward trend of bank lending is forecast to flatten out further compared to last year.

o While banks cling to conservative lending attitudes in fear of credit risk, there will also be a big falling-off in the demand for loans for firms' facilities investment and households' house purchases because of the economic downturn.

□ The scale of bonds issuance is expected to increase slightly from last year owing to the expanded issue of government and public bonds, but on the other hand, the demand for bond purchases is expected to decline below last year's level, led downward by life insurance companies and asset management

□ Foreign currency funds will generally show a net inflow as a reduction in the scale of net capital transaction outflows is expected together with a large increase in net inflows through current transactions.
◆ In fiscal policy, emphasis will be on underpinning an
□ The government's total budget (total expenditure basis, including government funds) in 2009 will increase by 10.6% over last
o The scale of the consolidated budget deficit, excluding social
security funds accounts, will widen greatly from last year to reach a ratio of 2.4% of nominal GDP.
It is considered that the 2007~2009 medium term inflation target (annualized average basis 3.0 ± 0.5%) can be attained.

Although the average rate of increase in the CPI exceeded the target during 2007 and 2008 when it was running at 3.6%, it is predicted to ease to 3.4% for the three-year period as a whole owing to the deceleration of the trend of price increases during 2009.
The inflation target for the period from 2010 will be established as early as possible* in view of the time lag of monetary policy.
The method of operating the inflation targeting system will be
Monetary policy will be operated with a main focus on supporting the recovery of economic activity and improving the flow of funds since prices are expected to be downwardly stable.

Toward financial stability, a monitoring system that tracks the markets closely will be reinforced and co-operation with the government and the central banks of the principal countries will be strengthened.
The Base Rate will be operated with a focus on improving economic activity and financial market conditions.

Positive steps will be taken to counter the possibility of a further deterioration of the economic situation in the course of an intensification of the financial turmoil by the lengthening of the business downturn.
The influence on the domestic economy of the trends of the international financial markets and world economic growth will be pre-emptively reflected in its operation.
The timing and the scale of Base Rate adjustment will be decided by closely checking the effectiveness of adjustments along with the economic and financial situation.
Positive encouragement will be given to the flow of funds to those areas to which the supply of credit has been constricted.
By way of open market operations, term RPs involving credit securities and the buying and selling of RPs with non-bank financial institution counterparts will be expanded.
Incentive for banks' lending to SMEs will be enhanced through adjustment of eligibility for Aggregate Credit Ceiling Loans and change in their allocation.
So that banks can expand their credit supply capacity, positive support will be given to their efforts to raise BIS capital
Funding support will be pursued for the government's planned 'Bank Capital Expansion Fund', as it has been
provisionally termed.
Plans are being studied to ease the credit crunch by, for example, the active supply of liquidity in the event of a further decline in the financial markets' function of funds
The monitoring system that closely tracks market movements will be further consolidated in order to promptly counter
A constant check will be kept not only on price movements in the financial market but on the conjuncture of supply and demand of individual financial markets and market
In particular, the pooling of information and consultation channels concerning the financial and foreign exchange markets will be expanded to handle the deepening interlinkages between these markets.
Policy coordination with the government on financial stability will be strengthened.

The pooling of information and materials with the government and financial supervisory authorities will be stepped up in order to detect at an early stage symptoms of abnormality in financial markets or at individual financial institutions.
It is intended that monetary policy should be conducted in harmonious coordination with fiscal and financial supervisory
Financial cooperation with the central banks of principal countries and multilateral financial agencies will be
Co-operation will be strengthened with the central banks of the United States, Japan, China and other major countries in order to overcome the global financial crisis.
We will also participate actively in the initiatives to prevent a recurrence of such a global financial crisis that are being orchestrated mainly through the G-20.
The Bank of Korea will improve the conditions for the use of its monetary policy tools including open market operations and the lending facilities.
A fungible issue system and a priority system for fixed rate tenders will be introduced so as to improve the efficiency of the issuance of MSBs.
Improvement of the system of collateral will be pursued including the expansion of the collateral eligibility of debt securities and the introduction of a haircut on the value of
The Bank will augment monetary policy predictability and
It will strengthen communication by way of a written statement setting out the direction of monetary policy.
The Bank will augment the stability and efficiency of the payment and settlement system.
It will strengthen settlement risk management in order to cope with the expanded participation of non-bank financial institutions in the payment and settlement system.
The Bank will alleviate some part of financial institutions' settlement funds burden through migration to the New BOK
Wire network (BOK Wire+).

The Bank plans to draw up proposals for improvement to ensure that the various policy instruments introduced in the course of coping with the financial turmoil fit in well with the existing monetary policy operating system.

In connection with the increase in the supply of liquidity, proposals will be studied for dealing in a market-friendly manner with the consequent expansion of the central bank balance sheet once the financial market situation evolves

2009-01-09.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to lower the Base Rate by 50 basis points from 3.00% to 2.50%.
In a related action, the Committee also lowered the interest rates on Aggregate Credit Ceiling Loans by 25 basis points from 1.75% to 1.50%.
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to lower the Base Rate by 50 basis points from 3.00% to 2.50%.
Domestic economic activity is slackening at a rapid pace.
The sluggishness of domestic consumption and investment has deepened further and exports have decreased significantly owing to the worldwide economic slowdown.
The downside risk to economic growth is considered to have been heightened by the persistent financial market unrest including the credit crunch.
Consumer price inflation has been decelerating steadily due to the fall in international oil prices and the impact of the economic downturn and the trend seems set to continue for some time.
In the real estate market, prices and transaction volumes have continued to decline.
There has been some moderation in the instability of financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices.
Firms are nevertheless having considerable difficulties in fund-raising since financial institutions, wary of credit risk, are very conservative in the operation of their funds.
Looking ahead, the Committee will do what is needed to improve liquidity conditions and to ward off the risk of a severe slowdown in economic activity.

2009-02-12.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to lower the Base Rate by 50 basis points from 2.50% to 2.00%.
In a related action, the Committee also lowered the interest rate on Aggregate Credit Ceiling Loans by 25 basis points from 1.50% to 1.25%.
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to lower the Base Rate by 50 basis points from 2.50% to 2.00%.
The pace of the domestic economic slowdown has accelerated.
Domestic consumption and investment have slackened off further and exports have decreased rapidly.
There is considered to be a greatly deepened downside risk to economic growth, which has been heightened by the worsening worldwide slump and the likelihood of the credit crunch persisting.

Consumer price inflation has been decelerating continually due to downwardly stable international oil prices coupled with the impact of the economic downturn and the trend seems set to last for some time.
In the real estate market, prices and transaction volumes have continued to decline.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have shown a generally stable pattern of movements.
Firms nevertheless still face considerable difficulties in fund-raising since financial institutions, wary of credit risk, are very conservative in the operation of their funds.
Looking ahead, the Committee will do what is needed to improve liquidity conditions and to ward off the risk of a severe slowdown in economic activity.

2009-03-12.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
(The following is an English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
Domestic economic activity has kept on declining due to the continued decrease in both domestic demand and exports.
The downside risk to economic growth is considered to have been greatly heightened by the deepening global economic slump and the international financial market unrest.

Consumer price inflation has moved higher under the influence of the won's depreciation, but the weakening of demand-pull pressures owing to the economic downturn will be likely to continue to moderate the pace of the increase.
In the real estate market, prices and transaction volumes have continued to decline.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have shown unstable movements.
Meanwhile, lending to the household sector and SMEs has increased in line with some moderation in financial institutions' attitude to lending.
Looking ahead, the Committee will do what is needed to ward off a severe slowdown in economic activity and to give support to financial market stabilization.

2009-05-12.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.

The pace of the domestic economic slowdown has moderated thanks to the narrowing scale of the decline in exports and proactive fiscal and monetary policy strive although domestic demand remains sluggish.
The downside risk to economic growth is thought to persist because of the sustained world-wide economic slump and the deterioration of labor market conditions.

Consumer price inflation has been decelerating thanks to stable international oil prices coupled with the easing of demand-pull pressures owing to the economic downturn, and this trend seems set to continue for some time.
In the real estate market, prices have shown upward movements in some part of Seoul and its surrounding areas in line with the increase in transaction volumes.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have continued to improve.
Lending to the household sector and SMEs has expanded steadily, but concerns about credit risk and the shortermism of market funds continue.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain accommodative policy stance for the time being and do what is needed to give support to economic recovery while helping bring about financial market stabilization.

2009-06-11.text: The Bank of Korea Monetary Policy Decision
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
(The following is an English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
The domestic economic downturn has flattened out thanks to the proactive fiscal and monetary policy strive.
The slump in domestic demand has moderated and production activities have improved.
The downside risk to economic growth is thought, however, to persist because of the rise in international raw material prices and the economic slowdown in the major advanced economies.
Consumer price inflation has been decelerating thanks to the easing of demand-pull pressures owing to the economic downturn coupled with the downwardly stable exchange rate.
The mild upward trend of real estate prices has continued.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have shown a stable pattern of movements.
Lending to the household sector and SMEs has expanded steadily, but concerns about credit risk and the shortermism of market funds have not been resolved.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain accommodative policy stance for the time being and do what is needed to bring about the continuation of the recent improving pattern of economic and financial movements.

2009-07-09.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
Domestic economic activity now appears to be emerging from its downward trend thanks to the proactive fiscal and monetary policy strive.
The slump in domestic demand and exports has moderated.
There still remains a high degree of uncertainty as to the economic growth path due to the rise in international raw material prices and the economic slowdown in the major advanced economies.
Consumer price inflation has continued to decelerate thanks to the easing of demand-pull pressures owing to the economic downturn.
The mild upward trend of real estate prices has been maintained.
Financial market price variables such as the exchange rate and stock prices have sustained their stable pattern of movements.
Bank lending has steadily expanded, mainly driven by mortgage loans.
Meanwhile, concerns about credit risk and the shortermism of market funds have not been resolved.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain accommodative policy stance for the time being and do what is needed to bring about the continuation of the recent improving pattern of economic movements and financial market stabilization.

2009-09-10.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.

Domestic demand and exports have staged a recovery and production has increased.

2009-10-09.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.

2010-01-07.text: Operational Direction of Monetary Policy in 2010

In accordance with the provisions of Article 6 of the Bank of Korea Act, we herewith set out the Monetary Policy Committee's decisions as to the direction of the operation of monetary policy in the Governor, the Bank of Korea
The inflation target for the 2010~2012 period has been set at a level of 3.0 ± 1% in terms of the year on year rate of increase in consumer prices.
In view of the high degree of uncertainty surrounding future price conditions, the permissible fluctuation range has been extended (± 0.5%p → ± 1%p).
Explanations to the general public as to the status of the operation of the medium term inflation target will be given by monitoring it on an annual basis.
Growth momentum is expected to accelerate in the Korean economy in 2010 amid continued price stability but a smaller current account surplus.

There is forecast to be an acceleration from last year in the pace of growth of the domestic economy as investment builds up led by facilities investment and exports stage a consistent recovery, offsetting the waning of the policy effect.
The rate of increase in the CPI will be running at around the 2% level generally, but there is the possibility of a gradual enlargement of upward pressures from the latter
The continued recovery in activity, the possibility of a rise in international commodity prices and increased charges for public utilities all act as factors making for rising prices.
The scale of the current account surplus will narrow somewhat, but a surplus of around 17 billion dollars for the year as a whole is still expected.
Market interest rates are forecast to respond to

underlying economic conditions such as economic activity
rather than the supply and demand of bonds.

◆ The scale of the increase in bank lending is expected to
be larger than that of last year, but improvement in the
borrowing conditions is seen less likely for firms with
poor credit ratings.

□ Market interest rates, responding more strongly to
underlying economic conditions such as economic activity
and the outlook for prices rather than the interplay of
bond supply and demand, are expected to trend smoothly

□ Bank lending is forecast to expand on a somewhat greater
scale than last year because of the increased demand for
borrowings arising from the recovery of activity, etc.

o There is nevertheless the possibility of certain SMEs being
affected by credit differentiation arising from the
reduction of financial support measures.

□ Foreign exchange liquidity is expected to be generally ample
thanks to the current account surplus and a continued net
inflow of foreign portfolio investment funds.

C. The Public Finances
◆ The degree of fiscal expansion is forecast to be somewhat

□ For the year 2010 the government's total budget (total
expenditure basis, including public funds) represents a 2.5%
increase over the main budget for the year 2009, but when
the supplementary budget is included, it constitutes a 3.3%

o The ratio to GDP of the consolidated budget balance
excluding social security funds is to be -2.9% as against
-5.0% in 2009, narrowing the deficit's scale.

◆ While maintaining the underlying stance of monetary
easing, the degree of accommodativeness will be
progressively moderated, taking into consideration overall
financial and economic conditions at home and abroad.

◆ Monetary policy tools will be improved and policy
communication strengthened to increase monetary policy
efficiency while efforts are also directed toward financial

□ The Base Rate will be operated for the time being in such
a way as to help sustain the trend of recovery in economic

o Given the high degree of uncertainty surrounding the
path of growth, the strengthening of private sector
growth momentum will be underpinned by maintaining
the stance of financial laxity.

□ Greater attention will be given as time goes by to the
possibility of the Korean economy experiencing imbalances
arising from the continuance for a prolonged period of the
policy of monetary accommodativeness.

o Adjustment of the degree of monetary policy easing will
be calibrated appropriately, taking into overall
consideration financial and economic circumstances
domestically and internationally.
Every effort will be made to bring about the convergence of the rate of increase in consumer prices upon the mid-point of the inflation target from a medium term perspective.
At the same time a very close watch will be kept on asset price movements.
Liquidity adjustment capacity will be enhanced by improving the instruments used in open market operations.
The system for the issue of MSBs will be constantly supplemented and improved so as to facilitate their more friction-free absorption.
Efforts will be continued to unwind liquidity support, which increased in the course of responding to the financial crisis.
Quotas under the Aggregate Credit Ceiling will be progressively adjusted downwards in the light of financial market conditions and the method of its operation will also be refined.
The outcomes and effectiveness of the new monetary policy operating framework put in place in March 2008 will be reviewed and a study made of measures considered desirable to complement it.
Factors with the potential to bring about financial instability will be continually checked.
International financial market risk factors, foreign capital inflows and outflows, the financial health of household balance sheets and such like will be closely monitored.
The function of checking the risk factors of non-bank financial institutions will be enhanced on the basis of the Bank's MOU with the financial supervisory agencies (entered into in September 2009).

Policy cooperation and the pooling of information with the government and the supervisory authorities will be strengthened to enable symptoms of systemic crisis to be detected immediately and prompt counter measures taken.
The Bank will strive to promote international cooperation for financial stability.

We intend to participate actively in discussions on financial stability in the G-20, the Financial Stability Board (FSB), the Basel Committee for Bank Supervision (BCBS) and other international forums.

The co-operative framework for financial stability could be made more secure through the successful holding this coming November of the G-20 Summit Meeting and Meeting of Ministers of Finance and Central Bank Governors and the BIS Meeting of Central Bank Governors.
Coping with external shocks such as abrupt capital outflows requires the further strengthening of the framework of international financial cooperation.
The efficiency and security of the payment and settlement system will be further enhanced.
The management of net settlement risk will be strengthened in response to the diversification of participant institutions and the expanded scale of utilization of retail settlement systems.
There will be a further strengthening of endeavors directed toward more effective policy communication including the improvement of resolutions on the direction of monetary
The newly introduced scheme for 'checking the outcome of the operation of the inflation target' on an annual basis will be further fleshed out.
The Monetary Policy Report and the Financial Stability Report, which are both published twice a year, are to be given more detailed content.

2010-01-08.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
Domestic economic activity has continued on a recovering trend, which is expected to be maintained.
Exports and domestic demand have continued to increase thanks to the progress of global economic situation.
There still, however, remains uncertainty as to the economic growth path due to the risk of delay in a full-fledged recovery of the major advanced economies.
Consumer price inflation has accelerated its rising pace, led by petroleum products and is anticipated to be further heightened temporarily by the influence of the heavy snowfalls.
The upward trend of real estate prices has slowed.
In the financial markets, long-term and short-term market interest rates and stock prices have shown a sharp run-up in line with the improvements in economic activity.
Mortgage lending has grown on a steady scale.
Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative policy stance for the time being in such a way as to help sustain the trend of recovery in economic activity.

2010-02-11.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
Domestic economic activity has continued on a recovering trend, which is expected to be maintained.
Exports and domestic demand have steadily increased and production has advanced briskly.
There still, however, remains uncertainty as to the economic growth path due to the risk of government debt crises in some European countries.
Consumer price inflation has accelerated its rising pace.
It is anticipated as likely to be stable, however, in reflection of recent international raw material price movements.
The upward trend of real estate prices has been blunted.
Financial market price variables such as exchange rate and stock prices have shown high volatility under the influence of international financial market unrest.
Mortgage lending has slowed due to the seasonal factors.
Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative policy stance for the time being in such a way as to help sustain the trend of recovery in economic activity.

2010-03-11.text:
The Bank of Korea
Monetary Policy Decision

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for
the intermeeting period.

(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy
Committee statement.)

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the
intermeeting period.

Domestic economic activity has continued on a recovering
trend, which is expected to be maintained.

Exports have risen and
domestic demand has steadily increased.

There still, however, remains
uncertainty as to the economic growth path due to the problems of
excessive government debt in some countries.
Consumer price inflation has decelerated somewhat.

It is
anticipated to be stable for some time given the recent movements of
international raw material prices.

In the real estate market,
transactions have remained inactive even though prices have risen
moderately.

In the financial markets, stock prices have rebounded and the
Korean won has appreciated in line with alleviation of international
financial market unrest, such as the government debt crises in some
european countries.

Mortgage lending has increased slightly due to
seasonal factors.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative
policy stance for the time being in such a way as to help sustain the
trend of recovery in economic activity.

2010-04-09.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.

In the global economy, emerging market economies have shown favorable performances, and the trend of improvement in the economies of major advanced countries, such as the US and Japan, has gathered pace to some degree.
However, there are latent risk factors present, including the government debt problems in some European countries.
Domestic economic activity has sustained a trend of recovery.
Even though construction investment has been somewhat inactive, exports have shown buoyancy, and consumption and facilities investment have steadily increased.
This recovery trend is expected to be maintained.
Considering the overseas risk factors, however, there still remains uncertainty as to the economic growth path.
Consumer price inflation has decelerated, led by the prices of services, and is anticipated to be stable for some time.
In the real estate market, the upward trend of prices has been blunted, with transactions remaining inactive.
In the financial markets, with the expansion in inflows of foreign portfolio investment funds, stock prices have risen, and long-term and short-term market interest rates have decreased.
The scale of the increase in mortgage lending has widened slightly due to the decline in lending interest rates, and the increase in the numbers of newly occupied and lotting-out apartments.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative policy stance for the time being in such a way as to help sustain the trend of recovery in economic activity, taking into overall consideration financial and economic conditions domestically and abroad.

2010-05-12.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the intermeeting period.
In the global economy, emerging market economies have sustained their favorable performance, while the economies of major advanced countries such as the US and Japan have shown an improving trend.

There is, however, an underlying risk that the international financial markets may be destabilized at any time, because no fundamental solution of government debt problems in certain Southern European countries has yet been reached.
The trend of recovery in domestic economic activity has become more evident.
Even though construction investment has still been inactive, exports have sustained their buoyancy, and facilities investment and consumption have steadily increased.
Labor market conditions have improved, led by the private sector.
This recovery trend is expected to be maintained.
Considering the overseas risk factors, however, there still remains uncertainty as to the economic growth path.
Consumer price inflation has accelerated temporarily, driven by the rise in farm and fisheries product prices due to unseasonably cold weather.
It is anticipated to be stable for some time, but the demand-pull pressure is expected to increase due to the recovery in economic activity.
In the real estate market, housing sales prices have exhibited weakness, dragged down by prices in Seoul and its surrounding areas, with transactions remaining inactive.

In the financial markets, with the expanded inflows of foreign portfolio investments, long-term and short-term market interest rates have decreased, however, the volatility of price variables has greatly expanded in response to the government debt problems in certain Southern European countries.
The scale of the increase in mortgage lending has remained almost unchanged, due to lackluster house transactions, even though lending interest rates have declined.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative policy stance in such a way as to help sustain the trend of recovery in economic activity, and in carrying out policy, it will take overall account of financial and economic conditions at home and abroad.

2010-06-10.text:
The Bank of Korea
Monetary Policy Decision

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for
the intermeeting period.

(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy
Committee statement.)

The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided
today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.00%) for the
intermeeting period.

In the global economy, emerging market economies have
sustained their favorable performance, while the economies of major
advanced countries such as the US and Japan have continued
improvement.

There is, however, an underlying risk that the
international financial markets may be destabilized at any time due
to the government debt problems in some European countries, and
that this could exercise an influence on the recovering trend in the
global economy.

The upward trend of domestic economic activity appears to
have been maintained.

Even though the increasing trend of domestic
demand such as consumption and investment has temporarily
faltered, exports have sustained their buoyancy and, led by the
private sector, labor market conditions have improved further.
This upward trend is expected to be maintained.

Considering
the overseas risk factors, however, there still remains uncertainty as
to the economic growth path.

Consumer price inflation has accelerated slightly, driven mainly
by petroleum products, and demand-pull pressure is expected to
increase gradually as the upward trend of economic activity
continues.

In the real estate market, housing sales prices have shown
weakness, dragged down by prices in Seoul and its surrounding
areas.

In the financial markets, price variables such as stock prices and
exchange rates have been highly volatile due to the government debt
problems in some European countries and the increase in the
geo-political risks surrounding the Korean Peninsula.

The scale of the
growth in mortgage lending has widened slightly in response chiefly to
reduced lending rates, even though house transactions have been inactive.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative
policy stance in such a way as to help the economy sustain its sound
growth on a foundation of price stability and, in carrying out policy,
it will take overall account of financial and economic conditions at
home and abroad.

2010-07-09.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to raise the Base Rate from 2.00% of its current level to 2.25%.

In the global economy, emerging market economies have sustained their favorable performance, while the economies of major advanced countries such as the US have largely continued their recovery trend.

The risks that international financial market unrest due to the government debt problems in some European countries and heightened volatility of growth in economic activity in major countries recur at any time cannot be ruled out.
The upward trend of domestic economic activity has been maintained.

Exports have held to their strong upward track, domestic demand such as consumption and investment has steadily increased and, led by the private sector, labor market conditions have shown an improving trend.
Domestic economic activity is expected to continue on an upward track, even with the presence of overseas risk factors.

Consumer price inflation has so far remained in a range of somewhat above 2% but a little below 3%, but in the future, upward pressures are expected to build continuously owing to the increase in demand-pull pressures associated with the continued upturn in economic activity.
In the real estate market, housing sales prices have declined in Seoul and its surrounding areas, while those in other areas have sustained a highly upward trend.

In the financial markets, long term market interest rates have increased, reflecting domestic economic conditions and, stock prices and exchange rates have exhibited high volatility due to the influence of overseas risk factors.
The scale of the growth in mortgage lending has widened in response chiefly to low lending rates, even though house transactions have been inactive.

Looking ahead, the Committee will maintain the accommodative policy stance in such a way as to help the economy sustain its sound growth on a foundation of price stability and, in carrying out policy, it will take overall account of financial and economic conditions at home and abroad.

In the global economy, emerging market economies have

2010-11-16.text: The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to raise the Base Rate from 2.25% of its current level to 2.50%.
(The following is an unofficial English translation of the Monetary Policy Committee statement.)
The Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Korea decided today to raise the Base Rate from 2.25% of its current level to 2.50%.
In the global economy, emerging market economies have sustained their favorable performance, and the economies of major advanced countries have largely continued their moderate recovery trend.
Looking ahead, the possibility of the heightened volatility of economic activity and exchange rates in major countries acting as a risk factor for the global economy cannot be ruled out.
The underlying upward trend of domestic business activity has been maintained.
Even though facilities investment has faltered, exports have sustained their buoyancy and consumption has increased.
And, led by the private sector, labor market conditions have shown an improving trend.
The domestic economy is expected to continue on an underlying upward track, even in the presence of external risk.
Consumer price inflation has increased further driven mainly by farm product prices.

Going forwards, inflation is expected to decrease somewhat due to the renewed stability of vegetable prices, however, upward pressures are expected to continue, being associated with the continued upswing in activity and the run-up in international raw material prices.

In the real estate market, the scale of the decrease in housing sales prices in Seoul and its surrounding areas has narrowed, with the upward trend of housing sales prices in other areas being maintained.

In the financial markets, amid continued inflows of foreign portfolio investment funds, stock prices have risen, the Korean won has appreciated, and in response chiefly to the high inflation rates, long term market interest rates have rebounded from their deep decline.
Mortgage lending has continued to increase on a relatively large scale, even though house transactions have been inactive.
Looking ahead, the Committee will conduct monetary policy in such a way as to help the economy maintain price stability, while sustaining sound growth.
In carrying out policy, it will take overall account of financial and economic conditions at home and abroad.

today to maintain the Base Rate at its current level (2.50%) for
